meta.title: Mental Maths for Kids: Number Sense, Not Tricks (Ages 5-10)
meta.description: Live mental maths course for kids ages 5-10: number bonds, decomposition and estimation build real number sense, the honest alternative to abacus. Free demo.
meta.level: Ages 5-10 (roughly grades K-5), all starting levels
meta.commitment: 2 live one-hour classes per week + 10 minutes of daily play-practice
meta.certification: Course completion certificate + stage mastery checks

program_overview.title: Mental Maths Mastery for Kids
program_overview.tagline: Number sense, not tricks. The speed follows, and it never expires.
program_overview.description: A 12-month live program that builds genuine mental arithmetic in children aged 5-10, the honest alternative to abacus franchises and 'Vedic tricks' courses. Trick-based speed impresses at demos and quietly expires: it bypasses understanding, so it cannot transfer to fractions, algebra or problem solving. We build number sense instead, numbers as decomposable quantities, number bonds, compensation and estimation, through games and puzzles a child genuinely enjoys. The result is a child who calculates fast because they see numbers clearly, and that clarity keeps paying through every year of school mathematics.
what_makes_this_different: Number sense over tricks: strategies a child can explain always beat procedures they can only perform
The speed is real and durable, decomposition, bonds and compensation transfer straight into fractions and algebra later
Games, puzzles and number talks: children wait for these classes, which is the engagement no drill sheet buys
Concrete-pictorial-abstract progression, the method behind the world's strongest maths systems
Explain-back in every class: what a child can teach, a child owns
One dedicated mentor who knows exactly which facts and strategies each child holds
A genuine on-ramp: graduates flow into our grade-level maths programs already ahead
One full interactive hour per class, twice a week, built for young attention spans with variety, not lectures

learning_path.phase_1: Number sense foundations: quantities, bonds to 10 and 20, and the part-whole thinking everything else stands on
learning_path.phase_2: The strategy toolkit: make-ten, doubles, compensation, and multiplication as structure, not chanting
learning_path.phase_3: Big-number courage: multi-digit mental strategies, fraction sense beginnings, estimation mastery and speed with understanding

career_outcomes: A child who volunteers answers in maths class instead of hiding
Arithmetic fluency that makes every later topic, fractions, percentages, algebra, feel lighter
Estimation judgment: the life skill of knowing when an answer looks wrong
A genuine head start into grade-level mastery programs and competition maths

phase_1_foundation.title: PHASE 1: Number Sense Foundations (Months 1-4)
phase_1_foundation.description: Before speed comes seeing: numbers as quantities that break apart and recombine. This phase builds subitizing, part-whole thinking and the bonds of 10 and 20 through concrete materials and games, tuned to each child's starting stage.

week_1.title: Getting to Know Your Number Sense
week_1.description: Playful diagnostic: which facts, strategies and confidence the child already holds, disguised as games
topics: Counting games that show how far a child counts with ease
Quick quantity flashes to see which small numbers are known on sight
Simple addition play that reveals the current strategy (counting on, known facts, or guessing)
Noticing confidence and comfort with numbers, not only right answers
Spotting facts already automatic, such as small doubles or bonds
Setting a friendly starting point tuned to the child, not the age
week_1.practice: Over a snack, ask your child to grab a small handful of raisins and guess how many before counting, then count together to check, keeping it light and playful.

week_2.title: Seeing How Many at a Glance
week_2.description: Subitizing and quantity sense: dots, dice and ten-frames, recognising amounts without counting
topics: Recognising 1 to 5 dots instantly without counting (subitizing)
Reading dice patterns as whole amounts
Meeting the ten-frame as a picture of numbers
Seeing 6 to 10 as a small group sitting on top of five
Flashing and hiding quantities to build quick recognition
Talking about how the amount was seen, not just the answer
week_2.practice: Roll one die a few times each evening and race to say the number before counting the dots, and whoever spots it first wins that round.

week_3.title: Numbers Are Made of Smaller Numbers
week_3.description: Part-whole thinking: every number is made of other numbers (7 is 5-and-2, 6-and-1, 3-and-4)
topics: Breaking a small number into two parts in different ways
Seeing 7 as 5 and 2, 6 and 1, or 4 and 3
Using fingers or counters to split a group across two hands
Understanding that the parts always rebuild the whole
Finding every way to make a chosen number
Explaining a split out loud in the child's own words
week_3.practice: Give your child 6 buttons and ask how many ways they can split them into two piles, writing each way down together like a treasure list.

week_4.title: Living on the Number Line
week_4.description: The number line as home: position, order, distance and the empty-number-line game
topics: Placing numbers in order along a line
Deciding which number is bigger from its position
Measuring the distance or jump between two numbers
Hopping forwards and backwards along the line
Meeting the empty number line as a thinking tool
Estimating where a number belongs before checking
week_4.practice: Draw a long line on paper with 0 at one end and 10 at the other, then take turns pointing to where numbers like 7 or 3 should sit.

month_2_3.title: Months 2-3: The Bonds That Power Everything

week_5_6.title: The Pairs That Make Ten
week_5_6.description: Bonds of 10 to automaticity, through games, never flashcard grind
topics: Finding both parts that add to make 10
Recognising 6 and 4, 7 and 3, 8 and 2 as ten-friends
Using ten-frames to see the missing part
Answering ten-bonds quickly through repeated play
Spotting a ten-bond hidden inside a larger problem
Building speed through fun, never flashcard pressure
week_5_6.practice: Play make ten: you say a number under ten and your child shouts the partner that finishes the ten, then swap roles after five turns.

week_7_8.title: Doubles and Their Neighbours
week_7_8.description: Doubles and near-doubles as anchor facts; adding 9 and 11 by compensation preview
topics: Learning the doubles up to 10 plus 10 as anchor facts
Using a known double to solve a near-double (6 plus 7 is double 6 and one more)
Adding 9 by adding 10 and stepping back one
Adding 11 by adding 10 and stepping up one
Seeing how one known fact unlocks several nearby ones
Choosing the closest double to lean on
week_7_8.practice: At bedtime, call out a double like double 4 for a quick answer, then a near-double like 4 plus 5, and let your child explain how the double helped.

week_9_10.title: Making Ten to Add Bigger
week_9_10.description: Bridging through 10: the make-ten strategy that turns 8+5 into 10+3
topics: Splitting the second number to fill up to ten first
Turning 8 plus 5 into 10 plus 3
Using ten-frames to see the bridge across ten
Knowing how much a number needs to reach ten
Adding the leftover part after reaching ten
Choosing make-ten when numbers sit close to ten
week_9_10.practice: With two ten-frames and some counters, set up sums like 8 plus 5 and let your child slide counters to fill the first ten, then read the answer.

week_11_12.title: Bonds to Twenty and Fact Families
week_11_12.description: Bonds to 20 and fact families: addition and subtraction as one idea seen from two sides
topics: Finding pairs that add to make 20
Linking a ten-bond to its twenty partner
Seeing a fact family: 13 plus 7, 7 plus 13, 20 minus 7, 20 minus 13
Understanding addition and subtraction as two views of one picture
Using a known adding fact to find its subtracting partner
Building all four facts from one number trio
week_11_12.practice: Pick three numbers that fit together, like 6, 4 and 10, and challenge your child to say all four facts they can make from them.

month_4.title: Month 4: Subtraction as Distance + Stage Checkpoint

week_13.title: Two Ways to Subtract
week_13.description: Subtraction two ways: taking away versus finding the gap, and when each is faster
topics: Taking away by counting back from the larger number
Finding the gap by counting up between the numbers
Deciding which way is faster for a given problem
Seeing subtraction as the distance between two numbers
Using the number line to picture both methods
Explaining which route was chosen and why
week_13.practice: Ask a shopping question like you have 10 and a toy costs 8, how much change, and let your child try both counting back and counting up to compare.

week_14.title: The Shopkeeper's Counting-Up Trick
week_14.description: Counting up for change: the shopkeeper's method every adult secretly uses
topics: Counting up from the price to the money paid
Using friendly stops like the next ten
Adding the small jumps to find the change
Seeing why counting up avoids hard take-away sums
Playing shop with real or pretend coins
Checking the change makes sense
week_14.practice: Set up a tiny pretend shop with priced items and let your child be the shopkeeper, counting up from the price to give you the right change.

week_15.title: Many Roads to the Same Answer
week_15.description: Mixed strategy play: number talks where children compare routes to the same answer
topics: Solving one problem in more than one way
Sharing a strategy out loud in a number talk
Listening to a different route to the same total
Comparing which method felt quickest
Respecting that different minds pick different paths
Naming the strategy used (make-ten, doubles, or counting up)
week_15.practice: Give one sum like 7 plus 8 and ask your child for two different ways to solve it, then you share a third way you thought of.

week_16.title: Phase One Check Through Play
week_16.description: Stage mastery check through games: solve it, explain it, teach it back
topics: Revisiting bonds of 10 and 20 through games
Choosing a strategy for each problem confidently
Explaining the thinking behind an answer
Teaching a strategy back to the mentor or a parent
Celebrating the facts now known by heart
Noticing which few facts still want more play
week_16.practice: Let your child be the teacher for ten minutes and show you one number strategy they learned this phase, explaining each step.
week_16.assessment: A relaxed review game covering number bonds, part-whole thinking and the early strategies, where the child solves a problem, explains it, and teaches a favourite method back, with no timing or pressure.

phase_2_strategies.title: PHASE 2: The Strategy Toolkit (Months 5-8)
phase_2_strategies.description: The heart of mental maths: named strategies the child chooses between, make-ten, compensation, doubling and halving, plus multiplication met as structure and arrays rather than chanted tables. Choice is the skill; speed is the side effect.

week_17_18.title: Adding Two-Digit Numbers in Jumps
week_17_18.description: Adding two-digit numbers mentally: jumps of ten, then the leftover ones
topics: Adding the tens first in one jump
Then adding the leftover ones
Using an empty number line to record the jumps
Holding the running total in mind
Solving sums like 34 plus 23 step by step
Checking the answer feels about right
week_17_18.practice: Call out a two-digit sum like 42 plus 30 during a car ride and ask your child to jump the tens out loud, then add any ones.

week_19_20.title: The Compensation Superpower
week_19_20.description: Compensation as a superpower: 39 + 26 becomes 40 + 25; subtracting 19 by taking 20 and giving one back
topics: Rounding a number up to a friendly ten
Adjusting the other number to keep the total fair
Turning 39 plus 26 into 40 plus 25
Subtracting 19 by taking 20 and giving one back
Understanding why the give-and-take keeps it equal
Choosing compensation when a number sits near a ten
week_19_20.practice: Give a near-ten sum like 29 plus 14 and let your child bump the 29 up to 30, solve it, then give the one back at the end.

week_21_22.title: Multiplication as Groups You Can See
week_21_22.description: Multiplication as groups and arrays, seen and built before recited
topics: Making equal groups with objects
Building rows and columns as an array
Reading 3 rows of 4 as three fours
Connecting repeated addition to multiplication
Seeing that an array can be counted two ways
Using the words times and groups of
week_21_22.practice: Arrange snacks or blocks into equal rows, like 3 rows of 5, and ask your child to find the total by seeing the groups rather than counting one by one.

week_23_24.title: The Clever Route Through the Tables
week_23_24.description: The clever tables route: 2s, 10s and 5s first, then doubling to 4s and 8s; 9s by compensation
topics: Learning the easy 2s, 5s and 10s first
Doubling the 2s to reach the 4s
Doubling the 4s to reach the 8s
Finding 9s by using 10s and stepping back a group
Seeing how known tables build the harder ones
Using skip counting as a rhythm for each table
week_23_24.practice: Practise one table a day by skip counting it like a chant on the walk to the door, starting with the easy 2s, 5s or 10s.

week_25_26.title: Taming the Trickiest Facts
week_25_26.description: 3s, 6s and the tricky squares; the handful of hard facts isolated and conquered
topics: Learning the 3s by skip counting and grouping
Reaching the 6s by doubling the 3s
Meeting the square facts like 6 times 6 and 7 times 7
Spotting the small handful of facts that feel hardest
Using a known nearby fact to reach a tricky one
Practising just the stubborn facts, not the whole grid
week_25_26.practice: Pick the one fact your child finds hardest this week and turn it into a silly rhyme or picture together to make it stick.

week_27_28.title: Division Is Multiplication Backwards
week_27_28.description: Division as the other side of multiplication; fact-family fluency through games
topics: Seeing division as sharing into equal groups
Linking 3 times 4 is 12 to 12 shared by 4 is 3
Building the multiplication and division fact family
Using a known times fact to answer a divide fact
Playing fact-family games for speed
Understanding remainders as leftovers, gently
week_27_28.practice: Share a small pile of objects equally between two or three plates and ask your child which times fact just proved the answer.

month_8.title: Month 8: Doubling, Halving and Strategy Choice

week_29_30.title: Doubling and Halving Magic
week_29_30.description: Doubling and halving chains: 16 x 5 as 8 x 10, the strategy that feels like magic and is pure structure
topics: Halving one number while doubling the other
Turning 16 times 5 into 8 times 10
Seeing why the answer stays the same
Choosing which number to halve to make it easy
Using this to multiply by 5 quickly
Chaining a halve and a double smoothly
week_29_30.practice: Offer a times-five problem like 14 times 5 and let your child halve the 14 to 7 and double the 5 to 10, then read off 7 times 10.

week_31_32.title: Strategy Tournament and Phase Two Check
week_31_32.description: Strategy tournaments: same problem, different routes, and the metacognition of choosing, plus stage mastery check
topics: Solving one problem several different ways
Deciding which strategy suits which problem
Explaining why a chosen route was quickest
Reviewing make-ten, compensation and doubling-halving
Owning the full multiplication and division facts
Thinking about one's own thinking
week_31_32.practice: Hold a friendly strategy tournament: give one problem and see how many different correct routes your child can find and name.
week_31_32.assessment: A relaxed review game across the strategy toolkit, where the child picks and explains a strategy for each problem and teaches a favourite one back, with no timing pressure.

phase_3_mastery.title: PHASE 3: Big Numbers, Estimation and Durable Speed (Months 9-12)
phase_3_mastery.description: The toolkit scales up: multi-digit mental arithmetic, the beginnings of fraction sense, estimation as judgment, and, now that understanding holds, genuine speed work. The course closes with each child measurably faster and, more importantly, able to explain every move.

week_33_34.title: Big Numbers Without Fear
week_33_34.description: Hundreds without fear: place-value jumps, friendly numbers and compensation at scale
topics: Jumping by hundreds and tens using place value
Adding to a friendly round number first
Using compensation on larger numbers
Adding numbers like 240 plus 130 in the head
Keeping the place values lined up while jumping
Checking the size of the answer makes sense
week_33_34.practice: During a walk, add house or page numbers like 120 plus 60, encouraging your child to jump the hundreds and tens out loud.

week_35_36.title: Multiplying Beyond the Tables
week_35_36.description: Mental multiplication beyond tables: 23 x 4 by splitting, 15 x 6 by halving-doubling
topics: Splitting a two-digit number into tens and ones
Multiplying each part then adding (23 times 4 is 20 times 4 plus 3 times 4)
Using halving and doubling on bigger products
Choosing the neatest way to split a number
Holding partial answers in mind to combine
Estimating first to check the result
week_35_36.practice: Give a problem like 21 times 3 and let your child split it into 20 times 3 and 1 times 3, then add the two parts.

week_37_38.title: Rounding to Get Close Fast
week_37_38.description: Rounding with purpose: getting close fast, and knowing how close is close enough
topics: Rounding a number to the nearest ten or hundred
Using rounded numbers for a quick estimate
Deciding how close an estimate needs to be
Comparing an estimate to the real answer
Rounding both numbers before adding
Understanding when an estimate is enough
week_37_38.practice: At the shop, ask your child to round a couple of prices and guess the rough total before you reach the till, then compare with the real total.

week_39_40.title: Does This Answer Make Sense?
week_39_40.description: The reasonableness habit: catching wrong answers by sense, the skill calculators cannot replace
topics: Checking whether an answer looks reasonable
Using an estimate to catch a big mistake
Spotting answers that are far too big or too small
Asking about how much this should be first
Trusting number sense over a typed answer
Explaining why an answer cannot be right
week_39_40.practice: Say a deliberately silly answer to an easy sum, like 3 plus 4 is 30, and let your child catch it and explain why it is wrong.

week_41_42.title: Halves and Quarters of Things
week_41_42.description: Halves, quarters and the fraction-of-a-quantity game, mental fractions from pictures
topics: Splitting a shape or group into equal parts
Finding half of a quantity, like half of 12
Finding a quarter by halving twice
Seeing halves and quarters in real objects
Understanding fair, equal shares
Reading a simple fraction from a picture
week_41_42.practice: Share a snack fairly and ask your child for half, then a quarter, of a small group of items, checking the shares are equal.

week_43_44.title: Half, Point Five and Fifty Percent
week_43_44.description: Fraction and decimal friends: one-half is 0.5 is 50%, the bridge built early
topics: Seeing one-half written as 0.5
Meeting fifty percent as another name for half
Linking a quarter to 0.25 and 25 percent
Understanding these are the same amount in different clothes
Spotting halves and percents in everyday life
Reading these friendly numbers with confidence
week_43_44.practice: Point out a half price or 50 percent off sign and ask your child what that means for a simple price, like half of 10.

week_45_48.title: Beat Your Own Record
week_45_48.description: Timed play now that meaning holds: beat-your-own-record ladders, never child-versus-child pressure
topics: Using known strategies quickly and calmly
Timing oneself gently to beat a personal best
Tracking progress on a personal record ladder
Choosing the fastest strategy for each fact
Building speed on top of real understanding
Racing only against yesterday's self, never a classmate
week_45_48.practice: Set a friendly one-minute challenge of easy facts and let your child try to beat their own score from last time, celebrating any gain.

week_49_52.title: Graduation and the Road Ahead
week_49_52.description: Graduation project: each child runs a number talk, teaching their favourite strategy, plus final stage mastery check and the road-ahead plan
topics: Choosing a favourite strategy to teach
Running a number talk for the class or mentor
Explaining each step clearly to others
Reviewing all the strategies learned this year
Celebrating durable, understood speed
Looking ahead to the next maths adventure
week_49_52.practice: Let your child teach the whole family their favourite strategy in a five-minute mini-lesson, using a real example.
week_49_52.assessment: A gentle final review where the child leads a number talk teaching a chosen strategy, revisits the year's key ideas, and celebrates progress, with a friendly road-ahead plan instead of a test.

faqs.question: How is this different from abacus or Vedic maths classes?
faqs.answer: Those programs teach procedures that produce fast answers, and the speed is real, but it lives outside understanding. It rarely transfers to fractions, word problems or algebra, and it fades when practice stops. We build number sense instead: strategies the child understands and chooses between. The speed arrives a little later and then never leaves, because it is made of understanding.

faqs.question: What age is right to start?
faqs.answer: Ages 5-10, and the mentor meets each child at their actual stage rather than their age. Five-year-olds start with quantity games and bonds; nine-year-olds might start at the strategy toolkit. The playful diagnostic in the free demo places every child accurately.

faqs.question: Will my child actually enjoy this, or is it drilling in disguise?
faqs.answer: Enjoyment is the engine of the whole method: number talks, strategy tournaments, beat-your-own-record ladders and games carry every class. Parents consistently tell us their children wait for these classes, which sounds impossible for arithmetic until you watch one. That is what the free demo is for.

faqs.question: My child counts on fingers past age 7. Is that a problem this fixes?
faqs.answer: Yes, and gently. Finger counting past grade 2 means number bonds never became automatic, so every calculation starts from scratch. We rebuild the bonds through games until known facts replace counting, without ever shaming the fingers on the way.

faqs.question: Does this replace school maths or run alongside it?
faqs.answer: Alongside, and underneath: number sense is the foundation school maths quietly assumes. Children in this course typically find school arithmetic suddenly easy, which buys the confidence that changes everything else.

faqs.question: How much practice is needed between classes?
faqs.answer: About ten minutes a day of assigned play, a game, a strategy challenge, a number talk with a parent. Small daily doses beat weekend marathons for fluency building, and ten minutes keeps it joyful.

faqs.question: What does the course cost?
faqs.answer: Small-group classes are ₹1,499 per month and private 1-on-1 mentorship is ₹4,999 per month in India; international students pay USD $100 per month for group and $150 for 1-on-1. Both include 8 live one-hour classes a month, recordings, and a free demo class before any payment.

faqs.question: Can we try it before paying?
faqs.answer: Yes. Every child starts with a free live demo class that doubles as the playful diagnostic, no card details, no obligation.

testimonials.name: Sonam Oswal, mother of Dhairya (verified Google review)
testimonials.text: My child Dhairya is really enjoying the classes. This is his first online class, and he eagerly looks forward to it. I can see his improvement.

testimonials.name: Shewta Singh, mother of Ishan (verified Google review)
testimonials.text: My son struggled with math for years. Integrating it into coding projects has transformed his understanding and confidence. Highly recommended!

projects_throughout_course: The child's own strategy book: every named strategy with their worked example
Number-talk leadership: each child regularly explains routes to the class or mentor
Beat-your-own-record fluency ladders, tracked across the year
Estimation journal: real-life guesses checked and calibrated
Graduation number talk taught by the child
additional_learning_resources.total_projects_built: A year-long strategy book plus 4+ fluency artifacts
skills_mastered: Doubling-halving and the clever tables route
Full multiplication and division fact fluency

weekly_structure.live_classes: 2 one-hour live classes, built with variety for young attention
weekly_structure.daily_play: About 10 minutes of assigned games or number talks
weekly_structure.family_involvement: Simple number-talk prompts parents can run at dinner

support_provided.live_sessions: 8 live one-hour classes every month, 2 per week
support_provided.mentorship: One dedicated mentor who knows the child's exact fact map
support_provided.progress_tracking: Stage mastery checks shared with parents
support_provided.flexibility: Slots move around school and family plans

certification.phase_certificates: Stage mastery check at the end of each phase
certification.final_certificate: Course completion certificate from Modern Age Coders

prerequisites.education: Ages 5-10; can count to 20 to start the youngest track
prerequisites.math_background: None assumed, the diagnostic finds the true stage
prerequisites.equipment: Computer or tablet with stable internet; household objects double as manipulatives
prerequisites.time_commitment: 2 live hours plus 10 minutes of daily play
prerequisites.motivation: None needed on day one, building it is the course's job

who_is_this_for.finger_counters: Children counting on fingers past grade 2, whose bonds never became automatic
who_is_this_for.maths_worriers: Children already saying 'I'm bad at maths', the belief this course exists to reverse
who_is_this_for.abacus_shoppers: Parents comparing abacus and Vedic programs who want speed that transfers and lasts
who_is_this_for.young_starters: Ages 5-6 whose parents want the foundation laid right the first time
who_is_this_for.quick_kids: Naturally strong children ready to go deeper than school arithmetic allows

career_paths_after_completion: Ready for the Elementary Mathematics Masterclass, arriving ahead
The fluency base that makes fractions and early algebra feel light
Competition-maths readiness for children who catch fire
A natural bridge into maths-through-coding for the builder types

course_guarantees.live_classes: Live, interactive classes with a real instructor, never pre-recorded videos.
course_guarantees.small_batches: Small batches only: group classes are capped at 10 students, with mini-batch (3 to 4 students) and personal 1-on-1 options.
course_guarantees.structured_curriculum: A structured, well-paced curriculum taught step by step, with hands-on practice in every session.
course_guarantees.doubt_support: Doubt support between classes over WhatsApp, so you are never left stuck.
course_guarantees.certificate: A course-completion certificate you can share.
course_guarantees.free_demo: A free demo class before you enrol, so you can decide with no pressure.
